main difference between iphone 16 and 17 design and build both models share a nearly identical aluminum frame and flat-edged glass design, with the same 0.31-inch thickness and vertical pill-shaped dual-camera module on the back. the iphone 17 weighs slightly more at 6.24 ounces vs. 6 ounces for the iphone 16. the iphone 17 introduces ceramic shield 2 glass on the front, offering 3x better scratch resistance than the iphone 16's ceramic shield. both include the camera control button and support for magsafe wireless charging. display size: iphone 17 has a larger 6.3-inch super retina xdr oled screen (thanks to slimmer bezels) compared to the iphone 16's 6.1-inch display, without increasing the phone's overall dimensions. refresh rate: this is the biggest upgrade—the iphone 17 features a 120hz promotion display with always-on support (variable refresh down to 1hz), making scrolling and animations much smoother. the iphone 16 is limited to 60hz. brightness and quality: both peak at 1,600 nits for hdr, but the iphone 17 adds a seven-layer anti-reflective coating for better outdoor visibility and reduced glare. performance and storage processor: iphone 17 uses the newer a19 chip (with integrated neural accelerators for ai tasks), which is faster and more efficient than the iphone 16's a18 chip. this enables better apple intelligence features and future-proofing. storage: iphone 17 starts at 256gb (up from 128gb on iphone 16), with a 512gb option. this makes the base model feel more premium without a price hike. both run ios 19 out of the box and support 5g, wi-fi 7, and face id. cameras rear setup: both have a 48mp main sensor and 12mp ultrawide with macro mode, but the iphone 17's ultrawide gets a sensor upgrade for sharper low-light shots and improved computational photography. front camera: iphone 17 upgrades to an 18mp truedepth selfie camera (vs. 12mp on iphone 16), with better low-light performance and portrait modes. video remains 4k at 60fps on both, with spatial video support. battery and charging battery life: iphone 17 lasts about 40% longer per charge—up to 25 hours of video playback vs. 17-18 hours on iphone 16—thanks to efficiency gains from the a19 chip and larger battery. charging: iphone 17 supports faster wired charging (up to 45w vs. 25w on iphone 16) and improved wireless speeds, though both top out at 15w magsafe.

excellent review really enjoyed it. so your videos from the first time you got the iphone 17. coming from a 14 pro. really wanted to get the iphone 17 air for the lightness and new design. especially as i was getting the iphone air for the same price as an iphone 17 at £799. saving £200 off its normal price. tired of carrying the big weight iphone 14 pro and the 17 pro is roughly the same weight. could live with all the other shortcomings of the air. so went to the apple store and spent quite a bit of time reviewing all the phones. unfortunately the iphone air fell down in in a few big areas for me personally, which are as follows: the speaker is awful. no loud volume or stereo sound no bottom firing speaker really was a dealbreaker. do listen to music on the speaker as well as making lots of calls on the speakerphone. the 14 pro has one of the best loudest speakers. the workaround is bluetooth speakers but when i do a lot of group calls the speaker quality has to be good. unfortunately this wasn’t. also, when watching videos while i’m doing other jobs around the house like washing up cleaning up. the volume and sand quality was really bad. no physical sim card support means can’t move sim card between phones when travelling. battery wasn’t an issue because i’m usually around chargers and also have an external second battery back up system. also felt the phone was a little too wide in my hand. even though i’ve got big hands just was not able to close it properly holding the phone. because of this am going to go for the iphone 17. which is a solid phone and nearly 30g lighter than the iphone 14 & 17 pro. next year when the iphone 18 air comes out with two speakers and two cameras then will purchase that. lastly, when it comes to the camera lenses. i take photos as a point in time reference. they don’t have to be great pictures but just good pictures. macro mode is important when taking close-ups. which the iphone air does not have macro mode but the iphone 17 does. so on the fly, the iphone 17 will take good pictures & videos. if i need better pictures and videos, then i can always take the 14 pro with me. or a better dedicated camera with a bigger optical zoom.

first, the switch 2 or steam deck or rog ally should not be compared directly with a home console like the ps5 or xbox. none of them are designed to deliver the level of performance comparable to home console. the switch 2’s success is driven less by raw power and more by its combination of portability, convenience, and strong first-party exclusives. it is not simply a case of the hardware being underpowered. in fact, the switch 2 is capable enough to handle demanding titles like resident evil 9 and pragmata better than the steam deck. look up youtube videos. the bigger limitation is often whether studios choose to release their games on nintendo hardware at all, or whether older titles receive proper updates and optimization for the new system. that is similar to how there are still many playstation-exclusive titles that you cannot access on the steam deck. that said more third-party titles are now making their way to the switch 2.

i feel like looking at it as a console is the wrong view; they were never in to compete with ps or xbox. it is a gameboy that you can comfortably play on a tv. and that was absolutely genius move by nintendo. as a pc gamer with more power than any console will have for a long time, the switch has been the perfect system: there when i want to pick it up and go and there when i want to melt into my couch. i don’t buy the “pretty” games for it (i frankly don’t buy them much at all; aaa is pretty sh*t these days), i get the niche, usually pixel art, indie stuff, a few nintendo titles, and retro snes and genesis games. there are a few outliers that are a real pleasure on the switch with my surround system, like ori. i did finally just get a proper controller for it (pdp, which should work with the switch 2 i have coming) and i will say that makes a huge difference. i’m actually finally enjoying botw. my switch will probably be retired to one of my desk monitors and for out-of-home use until one of my nephews is old enough to not break everything they touch. 😆

the switch 2 is honestly great! i got mine on launch day and was so excited when i unboxed and transferred my console data from the ns1 to the ns2. i was worried at first that i wouldn’t play mine that much due to being in college at the time and the fact i rarely played my nintendo switch 1. i only really ever grinded tetris, smash bros, and mk8 deluxe on the ns1. i am pleased to report that i play the absolute sh*t out of my ns2. the performance is great (although i hope they do make the new standard for games 45fps at least), i finally started breath of the wild and it feels like the way that game is meant to be played, and there are so many underrated games like dying light, assassins creed, and others that run so well. i have become a nintendo fan boy for sure and delved into the retrospectives thanks to my switch 2. some things could be better like the physical games and pricing but at least nintendo is consistent with the quality of the games they release. anyways if you made it this far into the comment i appreciate you and hope you have a blessed life! nintendo switch 2: 11/10 🔥🔥🔥

the premise of the video is predicated on the steam deck's new higher price remaining the same and other handhelds' prices somehow remaining stagnant. a thing to note is valve does not profit directly from steam deck sales. valve profits from people buying games from their steam store. the last thing they want is to increase the price of the steam deck. the fact that they increased the price means they were forced because of rising ram and ssd costs. imo, that's a very ominous sign for pc gaming broadly. if the ram crises continues, every other gaming device will continue to increase in price as well and the steam deck's perceived value will be directly tied to the pricing of gaming laptops, desktops, and other handhelds. it may very well wind up being the best "value" 12 months from now. if anything, i think this signals that pc gaming across the board may become to expensive for many people.

i would push back on the idea that the switch 2 selling way better isn’t a net positive for buyers. it’s not about caring that random other people bought it, it’s about what that popularity brings afterward. a huge install base means more support, more exclusivity deals, more third-party games, better ports, and developers being willing to try more experimental ideas because there’s a massive audience. if a console only sells a million units, publishers won’t invest much into it. but when a platform explodes in popularity, everyone wants a piece of that market. that’s the real benefit to the consumer: more choice, more variety, and a healthier ecosystem long term.

the best value is a legion go 1 which you can obtain used for $500. then if you install steamos onto it, it will be able to run most games at a resolution of 1600p using some combination of in game fsr & the linux port of lossless scaling. you will also be able to exceed 100 fps on most games by scaling down your resolution & running lossless scaling. but that 1600p resolution is twice the resolution of a steam deck. i just played both 007 first light at 1600p and mafia the old country at 1600p on a steamos legion go 1. you will exceed 30 fps minimum. but you’ll likely be able to push it up to 60fps tbh. at 1600p. if you understand how to use performance tools/enhancement tech. if you know how to utilize basic performance cheat tools then windows absolutely thrashes steamos in performance. for me the only real draw to steamos on a portable is the ability to run games at 1600p with just whatever frs is included in game. and not having to upscale with amd software to reach that resolution. so the legion go 1 is easily the #1 portable to run steamos off of. no question.

i have had the lcd steamdeck since almost launch, and decided to get the oled just last christmas (had no idea they would be so hard to get just 3 months later). since the purchase ofbthe lcd model, i have purchased 4 games through steam (ffx/x-2, ff12, new vegas, and trails of the sky fc). i never bought it thinking i am going to play any major aaa games on it. i am mostly a console gamer. the idea of getting the deck near launch was that i wanted to get into pc gaming and wanted the deck to be a companion in a pinch. it was never ment to be my main way to play. what makes it necessary for me is its incredibly incredible emulation abilities. for various market reasons, i have never been able to comfortably make the jump to pc gaming. so, my backlog of ps5/4 games and and almost infinite possibilities through emulation have made my oled one of my most prized possessions. want to stream a game from my ps5 while i am taking a shit, no problem. want to try and finally beat battle toads or adventure island with retroarch's rewind function (not cheating 😀), no problem. want to keep my sealed copy of certain other games from a certain company that uses red letters and has detachable things, no problem. my point is that my massive use case for the deck.does not include aaa gaming and has never been the main use case for me. and honestly in this market, i am so happy i have one. i have zero issues the way i use it if you know what i mean.

so: steam deck out of stock, people mad at valve because out of stock steam deck back in stock with higher prices because they cost more to make, people mad at valve because price is higher. steam deck sells out in less than 24 hours. people mad at valve because not enough stock. was also the same story with the controller - "$99 is way too much!" - sold out in 25 minutes. nobody likes to pay more for things, even when that cost is justified by higher costs to make (or quality) - but they damn well pay it because "expensive" is relative, while "nonexistent" is very not. the steam machine failed before because (1) third parties doing what was best for them and not valve (or the end user), and (2) it expected devs to port to linux, which was overly optimistic. the new steam machine is a reference model designed and made by valve directly, and *plays all of your old games unmodified* because of tech like proton that didn't exist the first time around. say what you will about valve, they aren't stupid. they make mistakes, but they learn from them.

friendly. i don't want to see keyboard and mouse button prompts when i boot up portal. i don't enjoy not having aim assist in battlefield when i do when i remote play my xbox on my ally. this thing is your fingers are and you can't get a good grip. this makes shoulder buttons veeeery awkward to hit. plus they are terrible membrane actuation and back buttons need to be mechanical.your fingers don't have dexterity like your thumbs. this thing screams cost cutting. it also looks awful.definitely designed by a comp sci grad rather than an actual designer. it looks like what people thought things from the future would look like in 1980. you're not gonna set this up in a best buy next to an ipad and not have people go ew. with that said, the screen is great and it plays games. steam remote play is better than ps remote play as it supports hdr and high refresh rate. neither supports 3d audio like xbox though. still a great buy for the right person but not as good as a switch 2. the fandom
